papers.title: Hypervelocity stars from star clusters hosting Intermediate-Mass Black Holes

abstract.text: Hypervelocity stars (HVSs) represent a unique population of stars in the Galaxy reflecting properties of the whole Galactic potential. Determining their origin is of fundamental importance to constrain the shape and mass of the dark halo. The leading scenario for the ejection of HVSs is an encounter with the supermassive black hole in the Galactic Centre. However, new proper motions from the \\\\textit{Gaia} mission indicate that only the fastest HVSs can be traced back to the Galactic centre and the remaining stars originate in the disc or halo. In this paper, we study HVSs generated by encounters of stellar binaries with an intermediate-mass black hole (IMBH) in the core of a star cluster. For the first time, we model the effect of the cluster orbit in the Galactic potential on the observable properties of the ejected population. HVSs generated by this mechanism do not travel on radial orbits consistent with a Galactic centre origin, but rather point back to their parent cluster, thus providing observational evidence for the presence of an IMBH. We also model the ejection of high-velocity stars from the Galactic population of globular clusters, assuming that they all contain an IMBH, including the effects of the cluster's orbit and propagation of the star in the Galactic potential up to detection. We find that high-velocity stars ejected by IMBHs have distinctive distributions in velocity, Galactocentric distance and Galactic latitude, which can be used to distinguish them from runaway stars and stars ejected from the Galactic Centre.

brief.tldr: [research paper] This paper models the ejection of hypervelocity stars (HVSs) from stellar binaries interacting with intermediate-mass black holes (IMBHs) in the cores of star clusters, introducing the first treatment of cluster orbital dynamics and Galactic potential propagation in the observable properties of ejected stars. Unlike HVSs from the Galactic Centre, these stars exhibit velocity and spatial distributions that point back to their parent cluster rather than radially outward, enabling observational discrimination between IMBH-origin HVSs and runaway or GC-origin stars via velocity, Galactocentric distance, and Galactic latitude distributions.

source_fields.title: Hypervelocity stars from star clusters hosting Intermediate-Mass Black Holes

source_fields.venue: Monthly notices of the Royal Astronomical Society
source_fields.journal_name: Monthly Notices of the Royal Astronomical Society
source_fields.sections_text: Introduction: This paper leverages Gaia DR2's precise astrometric and radial velocity data to identify and characterize unbound high-velocity stars, enabling a more accurate determination of their origins—particularly distinguishing Galactic Centre ejection mechanisms from other sources—and thereby improving constraints on the Galactic mass distribution and dark halo structure. Numerical setup: The numerical setup describes a series of scattering experiments involving binaries orbiting an intermediate-mass black hole, with initial conditions carefully chosen to ensure realistic pericentre distances, stellar collisions, and orbital dynamics, followed by long-term integration using a regularized N-body code. Discussions and Conclusions: Simulations show that high-velocity stars ejected from star clusters hosting intermediate-mass black holes exhibit distinct velocity, Galactocentric distance, and Galactic latitude distributions, with an ejection rate of $0.7–1.2 \\times 10^{-4} \\,\\text{yr}^{-1}$, offering a promising pathway to distinguish IMBH-driven ejections from the Hills mechanism and constrain the origin of high-velocity stars. Velocity distributions of ejected stars: The velocity distributions of ejected stars in IMBH-binary encounters are peaked at Hills velocities consistent with theoretical predictions, with peak velocity primarily dependent on the initial binary semi-major axis and IMBH mass (scaling as $v_{ej} \\propto M^{1/6}$), and showing broader distributions for larger IMBH masses. Ejecting hypervelocity stars from star clusters: Hypervelocity stars are ejected from star clusters hosting an intermediate-mass black hole (IMBH) in the core through binary scattering events, with their final velocities determined by the cluster's orbital motion and the Galactic potential, and their distribution influenced by the cluster's orbital parameters. Binary interactions with an Intermediate-mass Black Hole: Binary interactions between a star pair and an intermediate-mass black hole can eject stars at hypervelocities, with ejection speeds depending on the binary's semi-major axis, masses, and the black hole's mass, and the velocity distribution is well-described by a Gaussian centered on the theoretical prediction. High velocity stars ejected from the Milky Way Globular Clusters: This section compares two models for intermediate-mass black hole (IMBH) masses in Milky Way globular clusters—formed via runaway collisions or extrapolated from the M–σ relation—and uses them to predict the velocity distributions of high-velocity stars ejected from these clusters.

catalog_assessment.evidence: Purely theoretical modeling paper studying ejection mechanisms and distributions, no mention of actual observational data or object-level catalogs
